preamble: The transformation of a palatalized voiced velar plosive (/gʲ/) into a voiced palatal approximant (/j/) is a common phonetic shift, primarily driven by lenition. This process involves a weakening of the consonant's articulation, moving from a complete closure (plosive) to an open approximation (approximant). It is frequently triggered by the presence of front vowels or palatal glides in the phonetic environment, which induce palatalization of the velar consonant, setting the stage for its further approximantization.
phoneticEffects: Lenition (Plosive to Approximant), Palatalization (often as a conditioning factor or an intermediate stage)

examples.note: Old English /g/ before front vowels often shifted to /j/ (spelled 'y') in later English, implying an intermediate palatalized velar stage.
While 'give' retains a /g/ in Modern English, the Old English form with ġ (representing /j/ or /gʲ/) illustrates the historical tendency.
Written 'g' is often pronounced as /j/ before 'i' or 'ei'.
The initial /g/ in 'ganz' (standard German) becomes /j/ in the Berlin dialect, as seen in the abbreviation 'jotwede' (j.w.d.).
The initial /g/ in 'gut' and 'gegangen' (standard German) becomes /j/ in the Cologne dialect.

sources: What exactly causes palatalization of sounds like /g/ to /j/ in Old English, and why doesn't this happen in Romance languages? - Quora. (December 23 2025).
Is there a sound change from [ɡ] to [i] or [j]? - Linguistics Stack Exchange. (July 15 2020).
